data.title: Bank of Portugal sharply lowers 2026 growth forecast due to war in Iran, storms
data.description:
The Bank of Portugal on Wednesday sharply lowered the country's 2026 economic growth forecast to just 1.8% from the 2.3% predicted in December, citing the negative impact of the war in Iran and damage from recent storms in the mainland on activity.

That would mark a slowdown from the 1.9% expansion registered last year.

In its quarterly economic bulletin, it said "the abrupt and significant rise in energy commodity prices" due to the war in Iran was pulling down activity and putting upward pressure on inflation,"especially in 2026".

"There is high uncertainty, and an intensification or prolongation of the conflict implies significant risks," it said.

It said that the storms between the end of January and mid-February that strongly hit mainland Portugal "also affect the evolution of activity in 2026".

The central bank said it expected the economy to grow by 1.6% next year and by 1.8% in 2028.

Portugal's euro area-harmonised inflation is projected to accelerate to 2.8% this year from 2.2% in 2025.

data.title: ASOS Shares Surge as Turnaround Efforts Start to Pay off
data.description:
By Andrea Figueras

Shares in ASOS soared after the online fashion retailer started to show green shoots from its turnaround strategy.

For the six months through March 1, the company posted an on-year increase of around 50% in adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization.

The stock was up 14.5% at 243 pence in European morning trading.

The group is pushing ahead with a business reset, which started after Chief Executive Officer Jose Antonio Ramos Calamonte took the reins in 2022, while it tries to fend off rising competition in the fashion space.

"ASOS is making good progress on its strategy to improve the underlying profitability of the business," analysts at RBC Capital Markets wrote in a note to clients. However, the company operates in a highly competitive environment and more investments may be required to drive revenue, they added.

Meanwhile, the group said Wednesday that for its fiscal first half, gross merchandise volume--a key top-line indicator--dropped 9% compared with the same period a year prior, according to a pre-close trading update.

This was below estimates and reflects ASOS's limited participation in a highly promotional apparel market, Berenberg analysts said in a note. Despite the drop, the group pointed to a sequential improvement over the first and second quarters.

"We are constructive on ASOS's ongoing recovery potential," Berenberg said.

Green shoots should start to translate into better results in gross merchandise value over the group's fiscal second half, Peel Hunt analysts said in a research note.

"A return to revenue growth is the next milestone," they said.

Meanwhile, analysts at Panmure Liberum said that ASOS needs a return to sales growth to prove that the significant changes made to the operating model are resonating enough.

The company maintained its targets for fiscal 2026 as a whole and pointed to an improvement in new customer growth.

Write to Andrea Figueras at andrea.figueras@wsj.com

data.title: Europe's Militaries Test Green Fuels as Iran War Exposes Supply-Chain Vulnerabilities — WSJ
data.description:
By Yusuf Khan

European militaries are taking steps to boost their use of alternative fuels as supply shocks from the war in Iran accentuate the risks surrounding the continent's reliance on importing fossil fuels.

In the Netherlands, work is being ramped up to scale use of sustainable aviation fuel in all of its military's helicopters and jets for use as soon as next year, with the crisis in Iran posing a threat to its fuel sources. "The war in Iran and the crisis in the Strait of Hormuz underscore the need to accelerate efforts in energy resilience in general and enhance strategic independence from fossil fuels in particular," a representative for the country's Ministry of Defense said.

In the U.K., the Royal Air Force recently used hydrotreated vegetable oil to power its famed Red Arrows aircraft in a flypast over London. It has also run trials using the fuel to power operations on some of its military bases.

"Extensive work is already under way across defense on incorporating innovations that can create battlefield advantage and, consequently, reduce carbon emissions," a U.K. Ministry of Defense representative said. "The Royal Air Force continues to make progress on the use of alternative fuels, which have the potential to bolster operational resilience by reducing reliance on vulnerable supply chains."

The recent conflict in Iran has meant supply of oil and gas from the Strait of Hormuz has been dramatically curtailed, causing prices to rise sharply and countries to release oil from strategic reserves. It's a particular concern, as Europe gets most of its jet fuel from countries shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.

Alternative fuels sources such as biofuels or synthetic hydrocarbons have traditionally been seen as ways of greening hard-to-abate sectors, such as aviation. Biofuels include hydrotreated vegetable oil, such as those derived from used cooking oil and jet fuels made from cover crops. Synthetic fuels are produced from hydrogen and captured carbon.

Drop-in fuels, those that have the same chemical composition as traditional fuels, have been used in place of fossil fuels. All these forms of alternative fuel are increasingly being seen by European militaries as a way of securing fuel for operations, as worries over both supply and cost rise for fossil-based kerosene and diesel.

"When geopolitical pressure constricts around chokepoints, it demonstrates how much we rely on narrow supply routes," said Andrew Hall, divisional director of large and complex risk and climate risk and resilience at insurer Howden and a member of the U.K's armed forces for over three decades. "The lesson is not to panic and instead look at diversification."

On the commercial side, Germany's Rheinmetall late last year said it was developing a "Europe-wide network of several hundred modular e-fuel production plants," capable of producing 5,000 to 7,000 tonnes of synthetic, diesel, marine diesel, or kerosene per year.

"War readiness requires a resilient energy infrastructure. Maintaining fossil fuel supply chains will be challenging for European countries in the event of a defense situation," said Armin Papperger, CEO of Rheinmetall in a press release. "With the [e-fuels division] Giga PtX facilities, Rheinmetall and its partners are sending a strong signal of industrial transformation, climate protection and security resilience in Europe."

Experts say the push for alternative fuels by militaries has only hastened with the recent conflict. "There's an awareness that the fuel supply chain is something they need to look at and that has been growing, " said Irina Patrahau, strategic analyst and Chair of the Energy Security and Critical Minerals initiatives at the Hague Centre for Strategic Studies.

Patrahau added that most militaries in the region run on a single fuel concept — where both air and land operations can run off jet fuel. That itself, she said, can be synthesized using low carbon sources, such as through the HEFA method where vegetable oils are refined or synthetic fuels which combine hydrogen and carbon captured from other sources.

"It's an important long term consideration that needs to be taken now," said Patrahau. "If you invest in them now you want to have them in five years."

Sweden's military has been testing biofuels in some of the engines used in its fighter jets. France meanwhile has been testing a blend which uses 30% SAF combined with ordinary fossil fuel.

In the Netherlands, the Dutch military has committed to 20% of all fuel sources coming from biofuels by 2030. Next year, some form of SAF is going to be used in all helicopters and airplanes, it said.

"The crisis in the Strait of Hormuz confirms that the principles inherent to the MOD NL's Energy and Sustainability policy show a way forward, and that reducing reliance on fossil fuels reinforces the operational effectiveness of the Netherlands Armed Forces," a spokesperson from the Dutch Ministry of Defense told the WSJ Pro Sustainable Business.

That said, sources of biofuel remain scant, with militaries needing supply from civilian sources to ensure sufficient volumes for specialist uses.

In 2025 only 0.6% of flights used sustainable aviation fuels, with a lack of supply and higher costs still limiting uptake, according to the International Air Transport Association. The European Union has mandated that 2% of flights taking off from European airports use SAF, and is aiming for an industrial scale ramp up with 70% of flights using SAF by 2050.

The Dutch military said production of alternative fuels is near nonexistent in Europe, with it mostly imported from China. "This poses a strategic risk," the spokesperson said. "Only by stimulating and accelerating European production of SAF can alternative fuels wean European countries off of fossil fuel dependence without creating new dependencies." The military added that costs of European SAF are expected to be well higher than kerosene, even with the current Iranian crisis.

Still for clean energy advocates, diversifying fuel sources should be a no-brainer.

"It would be stupid to not consider that each country, each continent should be capable to have a synthetic way to produce its own fuel, to be at least self-sufficient when it comes in a situation where it's at war with other countries and it needs to be sure it gets enough fuel to defend itself," said Pierre-Etienne Franc, CEO of Hy24 a clean-hydrogen specialist investment manager, and an advocate of developing synthetic fuels with hydrogen.

Franc added that arguments for developing bio and synthetic fuels go beyond climate, when it comes to military uses. "It's easier to have sovereign green energy sources than to have sovereign fossil energy sources if you don't have any fossil resources."

Write to Yusuf Khan at yusuf.khan@wsj.com

data.title: Merck boosts cancer portfolio with $6.7 billion buyout of Terns Pharma
data.description: Merck said on Wednesday it would buy biotech Terns Pharma in a deal valued at up to $6.7 billion, as the drugmaker races to bolster its cancer pipeline ahead of the looming patent loss for its blockbuster therapy Keytruda.

data.title: Merck Reaches Nearly $6 Billion Deal for Cancer Biotech Terns — WSJ
data.description:
By Peter Loftus

Merck has reached a nearly $6 billion cash deal to buy the cancer biotech Terns Pharmaceuticals and its promising leukemia treatment.

If it proves to work safely, the experimental drug would give Merck a boost as the company prepares for its top-selling drug, Keytruda, to lose patent protection.

Under the terms, Merck would pay $53 a share for Terns, Merck said Wednesday. The deal is worth $5.7 billion including the cash that Terns has on hand.

Terns, of Foster City, Calif., has been developing pills to treat cancer, as well as obesity and metabolic-liver diseases. Its crown jewel is a pill to treat a blood cancer known as chronic myeloid leukemia. Last year Terns reported encouraging study results for the medicine.

Wall Street analysts said the drug could be an eventual blockbuster, with Truist Securities estimating as much as $2.3 billion in peak adjusted annual sales.

Merck, of Rahway, N.J., is a cancer-drug powerhouse with a market value of over $285 billion. Its Keytruda immunotherapy, which notched around $31.7 billion in sales last year, is among the world's top sellers.

Keytruda will lose the protection of its main patent in 2028, which could cost the company a big chunk of its revenue.

Merck Chief Executive Robert Davis said at the J.P. Morgan Healthcare Conference in January that the company has been largely focused on deals of up to $15 billion in size.

Write to Peter Loftus at Peter.Loftus@wsj.com

data.title: China Eastern Airlines to Buy Airbus Jets Worth $15.8 Billion — Update
data.description:
By Kimberley Kao

China Eastern Airlines agreed to buy 101 Airbus A320neo jets in a deal worth about $15.8 billion.

The Chinese airline said in a stock exchange filling on Wednesday that the purchase has a total list price of about 108.89 billion yuan, equivalent to $15.80 billion. The jets are scheduled to be delivered in batches from 2028 to 2032, China Eastern said.

The airline said the purchase is part of the company's strategy to plan and reserve fleets in advance, and is pending the Chinese government's approval.

Airbus has granted price concessions on the purchase, China Eastern said, adding that the actual purchase price will be lower than the list price.

The transaction will be paid in installments and isn't expected to significantly affect the company's cash flow and operations, it said.

The aircraft deal is the latest win for Airbus out of China.

In December, Air China, Spring Airlines, Juneyao Air, China Express Airlines and China Aircraft Leasing Group announced Airbus deals totaling 148 jets in a flurry of year-end orders.

Flag carrier Air China led the orders, with a purchase agreement for 60 Airbus A320neo jets worth about $9.5 billion.

China is the largest single country market for Airbus in the world in terms of fleet, according to the manufacturer. Airbus has projected strong demand for aircraft in China, and expects the market to take delivery of about 9,570 new jets in total over the next two decades. Airbus has said previously that it is taking China's homegrown plane maker Comac "very seriously" as a competitor.

The European plane maker has been facing supply-chain issues, partly due to shortages of Pratt & Whitney engines, which has forced it to slow production of its bestselling A320 jets this year.

Write to Kimberley Kao at kimberley.kao@wsj.com

data.title: Kuaishou Continues Earnings Growth Amid AI Push
data.description:
By Jason Chau

Kuaishou Technology, China's second-largest short-video platform, delivered broadly in-line quarterly and annual results, supported by revenue growth in its core advertising and e-commerce businesses as its continued its artificial-intelligence push.

Fourth-quarter net profit jumped 32% from a year earlier to 5.23 billion yuan, equivalent to $758.8 million. Adjusting for share-based compensation expenses and net fair-value changes on investments, profit rose 16% during the period.

Quarterly revenue also increased, climbing 12% to 39.57 billion yuan, the Beijing-based company said Wednesday.

Analysts had expected net profit of 5.38 billion yuan on revenue of 38.83 billion yuan, according to an LSEG consensus estimate.

The company attributed the increase in revenue to growth in its online marketing services, e-commerce segment and its AI business.

The results could help revive sentiment on the stock, which has lost momentum in recent months amid heightened market volatility. In 2025, Kuaishou's Hong Kong-listed shares surged nearly 55%, driven by strong investor optimism around the potential of its video-generation model, Kling AI, and the broader Chinese technology sector.

The company, known as the second-largest short video platform in China behind TikTok's sister app, Douyin, has continued to grow its daily active users, supported by a user base concentrated in lower-tier cities and rural areas. Despite intense competition from rivals like ByteDance, its average daily active users rose to 410.2 million last year from 399.4 million in 2024.

Its top and bottom lines for the full year also largely met market expectations, with net profit rising 21% to 18.62 billion yuan and revenue increasing 13% to 142.78 billion yuan. Adjusted net profit was 17% higher.

Overall, analysts have been positive on Kuaishou's outlook. The company's large user base, diversified revenue streams and prudent financial management should continue to support earnings, said CreditSights analysts.

S&P analysts said they expect its core e-commerce business, which contributes more than 40% of the company's revenue and over half of its profit, to remain a key growth driver this year.

"Kuaishou's greater proportion of rural-area users cushions it from the fierce competition surrounding instant deliveries in higher-tier cities," they wrote in a note.

Investors, meanwhile, are monitoring the progress in its AI technology. The company, which released its latest Kling 3.0 model in early February, said its AI tools now serve more than 60 million creators globally, with use cases across movies, animation and advertising.

Kling AI's revenue exceeded 340 million yuan in the fourth quarter, Kuaishou said.

While direct monetization of Kling remains limited, Kuaishou's AI capabilities are expected to support earnings growth in the coming years by reducing production and advertising costs and accelerating content creation, S&P analysts said.

Write to Jason Chau at jason.chau@wsj.com
